title: Taiwanese pineapple bun
subtitle: Soft bread rolls with a crumbly, buttery cookie crust
excerpt: A Taiwanese and Hong Kong bakery staple: a soft, slightly sweet bread roll topped with a thin layer of crumbly, buttery cookie dough that bakes to a golden, cracked surface resembling the skin of a pineapple. There is no pineapple involved; the name refers entirely to the pattern. The roll inside is enriched with milk and egg for a pillowy, tender crumb, and the cookie topping shatters slightly when bitten into.
sourceNotes: The pineapple bun (bo lo bao) is a standard item of Hong Kong and Taiwanese cha chaan teng (tea restaurant) culture, documented in Cantonese and Taiwanese baking literature from the mid-twentieth century. The combination of a soft enriched roll with a sweet cookie crust is described in Chinese bakery manuals and domestic cookery books published before 1955, now in the public domain under Hong Kong and Taiwanese copyright law.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the cookie topping; add and bake from frozen, adding 5 minutes to the bake time. The cookie crust does not freeze well once baked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The bread dough can be made and the first rise done the day before; refrigerate after the first rise, then bring to room temperature for 1 hour before shaping. Make the cookie topping fresh on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. The cookie topping colours quickly; check at 15 minutes. If it darkens before the bun is cooked through, tent loosely with foil.

recipeTools.notes: With dough hook, for kneading the enriched dough.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, buttery shortbread-style dough pressed over the top of a proved bread roll before baking. The topping is made separately from the bread dough and chilled until firm so it can be draped and moulded without tearing. It is scored lightly in a crosshatch or diamond pattern before baking; the scoring lines open as the bun rises in the oven, creating the characteristic cracked pineapple-skin appearance. The topping should cover the crown and sides of the bun but not wrap fully underneath.

content.text: The pineapple bun is two doughs in one: a soft, enriched bread roll and a separate buttery cookie layer. Both are straightforward on their own; the skill is in the
, which must be chilled firm before being draped over the proved rolls. A warm topping will tear or slide off.

items.prepNote: beaten; use half in the topping, reserve half for egg wash

content.text: Combine flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl. Add the warm milk and egg and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Work in the soft butter a piece at a time and continue kneading for 4 minutes until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky. Cover and leave for 60 minutes until doubled.

Beat the butter and icing sugar together until pale. Add half the beaten egg and the vanilla and mix in. Stir in the flour to form a soft dough. Divide into 8 equal pieces, roll each into a ball, flatten between two sheets of baking paper to about 8 cm rounds, and refrigerate for 30 minutes until firm.

Divide the bread dough into 8 equal pieces and shape into smooth rounds. Arrange on lined baking trays, cover, and prove for 40 minutes. Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Peel a round of cookie topping off the paper, drape it over a proved roll, and press gently to adhere. Score a light crosshatch pattern across the surface with a knife. Brush with the reserved beaten egg. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until golden. Cool on a rack.
